content.body:
Mole: 6.02×10²³ particles (Avogadro's number, L)

Molar mass M: mass of 1 mole in g/mol
= relative atomic/molecular mass in grams

Moles = mass/molar mass
n = m/M
m = nM

Moles of gas at STP: n = V/22.4 (litres)
Concentration: c = n/V(litres)
n = cV

content.title: Mole Calculations
content.body:
Moles of H₂O in 36g:
M(H₂O) = 2(1)+16 = 18 g/mol
n = 36/18 = 2 mol

Mass of 0.5 mol NaCl:
M(NaCl) = 23+35.5 = 58.5 g/mol
m = 0.5×58.5 = 29.25g

Molecules in 2 mol CO₂:
N = 2 × 6.02×10²³ = 1.204×10²⁴ molecules

content.title: Concentration
content.body:
2 mol NaOH dissolved in 500cm³
V = 0.5L
c = 2/0.5 = 4 mol/L (4M)

250cm³ of 0.1M HCl:
n = 0.1 × 0.25 = 0.025 mol = 0.025×36.5 = 0.91g HCl

questions.question: Avogadro's number is
questions.explanation: Avogadro's number: 6.022×10²³ particles per mole

questions.question: Molar mass of CO₂ (C=12, O=16)
Moles in 44g of CO₂ (M=44)
Mass of 3 mol H₂O (M=18)
Concentration: 0.1 mol in 2 litres
Moles of Na₂SO₄ (M=142) in 71g
Volume of 0.5 mol gas at STP
Number of molecules in 0.25 mol
0.2M HCl, 100cm³. Moles of HCl =
Empirical formula mass of CH₂O = 30. Molecular mass = 180. Molecular formula =
questions.explanation: 180/30=6. Multiply empirical formula by 6: C₆H₁₂O₆ (glucose)

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Stoichiometry uses mole ratios from balanced equation:
aA + bB → cC + dD
moles A : moles B : moles C : moles D = a : b : c : d

Limiting reagent: reactant that runs out first
(determines max product)

Excess reagent: left over after reaction

% yield = (actual yield/theoretical yield) × 100
% purity = (pure mass/total mass) × 100

Atom economy = (desired product mass/all products mass) × 100

content.title: Stoichiometry Example
content.body:
2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O
4 mol H₂ + 3 mol O₂:
H₂ needs O₂ = 4/2 = 2 mol
Available O₂ = 3 mol (excess)
H₂ is limiting reagent
H₂O produced = 4 mol (1:1 with H₂)

content.title: Percentage Yield
content.body:
N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃
1 mol N₂ + 3 mol H₂: theoretical yield = 2 mol NH₃ = 34g
Actual yield obtained = 28g
% yield = 28/34 × 100 = 82.4%

questions.question: In 2H₂+O₂→2H₂O, moles H₂O from 4 mol O₂ =
questions.explanation: Ratio O₂:H₂O = 1:2. 4 mol O₂ → 8 mol H₂O

questions.question: Limiting reagent is
options: reactant that runs out first limiting product formed
questions.answer: reactant that runs out first limiting product formed
questions.explanation: Limiting reagent consumed first — determines maximum amount of product

questions.question: Fe+S→FeS. 3mol Fe + 2mol S. Limiting reagent =
questions.explanation: Fe needs S in 1:1 ratio. 3mol Fe needs 3mol S but only 2mol available. S is limiting

questions.question: % yield formula
questions.explanation: % yield=(actual yield/theoretical maximum yield)×100%

questions.question: Theoretical yield 50g, actual 40g. % yield =
Atom economy favours reactions where
options: all atoms in reactants end up in desired product
questions.answer: all atoms in reactants end up in desired product
questions.explanation: High atom economy: less waste, more sustainable. All reactant atoms incorporated into desired product

questions.question: CaCO₃→CaO+CO₂. Atom economy for CaO =
questions.explanation: M(CaO)=56, M(CaCO₃)=100. Atom economy=56/100×100=56%

questions.question: Excess reagent is
questions.explanation: Excess: more than stoichiometrically required. Some remains after limiting reagent is consumed

questions.question: 2g H₂ + 32g O₂ → H₂O (M_H₂=2,M_O₂=32). Limiting reagent?
questions.explanation: n(H₂)=1mol, n(O₂)=1mol. Ratio needed H₂:O₂=2:1. 1mol H₂ needs 0.5mol O₂ — O₂ is actually in excess. H₂ limits

questions.question: Industrial processes want high atom economy because
options: less waste reduces cost and environmental impact
questions.answer: less waste reduces cost and environmental impact
questions.explanation: Green chemistry principle: high atom economy = less unwanted byproducts = lower cost, less pollution

questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?

questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

content.body:
Empirical formula: simplest whole number ratio of atoms
Molecular formula: actual number of atoms

Finding empirical formula from % composition:
1. Assume 100g → mass = %
2. Divide each mass by molar mass → moles
3. Divide by smallest → ratio
4. Round to whole numbers (×2 if needed)

Molecular formula:
MF = EF × n
n = molecular mass / empirical formula mass

content.title: Molecular Formula
content.body:
EF=CH₂O (M=30), actual M=180
n=180/30=6
MF = C₆H₁₂O₆ (glucose!)

questions.question: Empirical formula shows
questions.explanation: EF: simplest ratio. H₂O₂ has EF=HO; C₆H₁₂O₆ has EF=CH₂O

questions.question: Compound 75% C, 25% H. EF =
EF=CH₂, M=56. Molecular formula?
questions.explanation: M(CH₂)=14. n=56/14=4. MF=C₄H₈

questions.question: Combustion analysis: burning organic compound and measuring CO₂ and H₂O gives
options: C and H content (from CO₂ and H₂O masses)
questions.answer: C and H content (from CO₂ and H₂O masses)
questions.explanation: Combustion: C→CO₂, H→H₂O. Masses of CO₂ and H₂O reveal C and H in original compound

questions.question: Compound 85.7%C, 14.3%H. EF =
Molecular formula can be same as empirical formula when
questions.explanation: When n=1, MF=EF. Examples: H₂O, NaCl, CO₂

questions.question: Calculating moles step in empirical formula: divide mass by
questions.explanation: Moles = mass/molar mass. This converts grams to moles for each element

questions.question: After finding mole ratio 1.5:1:3, you should
questions.explanation: When ratio contains 0.5 (½), multiply all by 2 to get whole numbers

questions.question: Magnesium burns in oxygen. 0.48g Mg gives 0.80g MgO. Empirical formula (Mg=24,O=16)?
What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Mole Calculations. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Mole Calculations concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of chemistry.
Core Theory

This section of Mole Calculations builds on foundational knowledge. In chemistry, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Mole Calculations. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Mole Calculations. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Mole Calculations include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Mole Calculations. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the chemical formula of water?
questions.explanation: Water consists of 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom bonded covalently. Formula: H₂O.

questions.question: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: Carbon has 6 protons (atomic number = 6). Its most common isotope has mass number 12 (6p + 6n).

questions.question: What is the pH of a neutral solution?
questions.explanation: pH 7 is neutral. pH < 7 is acidic; pH > 7 is alkaline. Water has pH ≈ 7.

questions.question: What type of bond forms in NaCl?
questions.explanation: Sodium gives its outer electron to chlorine, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions held by electrostatic attraction — an ionic bond.

questions.question: What is the molar mass of CO₂? (C=12, O=16)
questions.explanation: CO₂: C=12, 2×O=32. Molar mass = 12 + 32 = 44 g/mol.

questions.question: What happens in a neutralisation reaction?
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: Acid + Base → Salt + Water. Example: HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O.

questions.question: Avogadro's number is approximately:
questions.explanation: Avogadro's constant = 6.02 × 10²³. One mole of any substance contains this many particles.

questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
Different elements with same mass number
Atoms with different numbers of protons
questions.answer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element (same proton number) but different neutron numbers, giving different mass numbers.

questions.question: In electrolysis of water, which gas forms at the cathode?
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode), hydrogen ions gain electrons and are reduced to H₂ gas: 2H⁺ + 2e⁻ → H₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
questions.explanation: Oxidation Is Loss, Reduction Is Gain — remember 'OIL RIG'. Oxidation = loss of electrons; reduction = gain of electrons.

questions.question: What is the formula for calculating moles?
questions.explanation: Number of moles = mass (g) ÷ molar mass (g/mol). This is the fundamental mole equation.

questions.question: Which gas is produced when zinc reacts with dilute sulphuric acid?
questions.explanation: Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂. Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series displace hydrogen from dilute acids.

questions.question: What is a covalent bond?
options: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.answer: Sharing of electron pairs between atoms
questions.explanation: In a covalent bond, two atoms share one or more pairs of electrons. Common in non-metal compounds like H₂O, CO₂, CH₄.

questions.question: What does the rate of reaction measure?
options: How fast reactants are converted to products
The energy released in a reaction
questions.answer: How fast reactants are converted to products
questions.explanation: Rate of reaction = change in concentration (or amount) of reactant/product per unit time.

questions.question: What is the effect of a catalyst on a reaction?
questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).

questions.explanation: A catalyst increases reaction rate by providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. It is not consumed.

questions.question: Which type of organic compound has the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂?
questions.explanation: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂. They are saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds.

questions.question: What is the first ionisation energy?
options: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
Energy released when an electron is added
questions.answer: Energy to remove one mole of electrons from one mole of gaseous atoms
questions.explanation: First ionisation energy: X(g) → X⁺(g) + e⁻. It is the energy needed to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom.

questions.question: What is Le Chatelier's Principle?
options: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
Temperature has no effect on equilibrium
questions.answer: If a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's Principle: A system at equilibrium responds to a change (temperature, pressure, concentration) by shifting to counteract it.

questions.question: What does 'exothermic' mean?
options: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.answer: The reaction releases heat energy to surroundings
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions release energy (ΔH < 0). Products have less energy than reactants. Examples: combustion, neutralisation.

questions.question: What is Boyle's Law?
options: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.answer: At constant temperature, pressure × volume = constant (P₁V₁ = P₂V₂)
questions.explanation: Boyle's Law: P ∝ 1/V at constant temperature. Doubling pressure halves volume (for ideal gases).
